question_title: Will the interest in “hungary election” change between 2026-04-14 and 2026-04-25 according to Google Trends?
summary: The Hungarian parliamentary election on April 12, 2026 (Orbán's historic defeat after 16 years) created a massive Google Trends spike to 100 that has already decayed to 14 by April 14. The term's baseline over the prior 12 months was essentially 0-1. By April 25 (11 days later), the value is overwhelmingly likely to have decayed further toward baseline (1-5 range), well below the "Decreases" threshold of <11. The "Doesn't change" band (11-17) requires sustained elevated interest for 11 more days despite the steep decay trajectory. "Increases" (>17) requires a new spike above current levels, needing an unexpected major event. While government formation proceedings and potential Trump commentary could generate some follow-up interest, these are lower-salience for US audiences and unlikely to sustain the current level.

factors.description: Post-election spike decay is extremely rapid: 100→40→14 in 2 days. Exponential decay models project values of 0-3 by April 25.
Zero baseline: 'hungary election' registered 0-1 in US Google Trends for 10+ months before the election spike. Post-election interest returns to this equilibrium.
The 'Doesn't change' band (11-17) is narrow and requires interest to stay near its current elevated level for 11 more days, contradicting the steep decay already observed.
Government formation process (parliament convenes by May 12) may generate some follow-up coverage, but these are lower-salience events for US audiences.
Potential for Trump/US involvement angle: Trump endorsed Orbán, and his defeat could prompt commentary creating a secondary spike. Speculative.
SerpAPI vs pytrends measurement uncertainty (±1-3 points), but the expected magnitude of decline far exceeds measurement error.

reasoning:
**Forecast: "Decreases" at 80%**

Key reasoning:

- **Current state**: The Hungarian election (Apr 12) created a massive spike to 100. By Apr 14, it's already at 14 — an 86% decline in 2 days.
- **Baseline**: This term had near-zero US interest (0-1) for over 10 months before the election. The spike was a one-time event.
- **Threshold math**: "Decreases" requires Apr 25 < 11. Given the steep decay and near-zero baseline, values of 1-5 are most likely by Apr 25.
- **"Doesn't change" (11-17)** would require sustained elevated interest for 11 more days despite rapid decay — unlikely without a major new catalyst.
- **"Increases" (>17)** would need a new spike exceeding current levels — possible only with an unexpected major event (e.g., Trump commentary, constitutional crisis).

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| Decreases | 80% |
| Doesn't change | 12% |
| Increases | 8% |
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “hungary election” change between 2026-04-14 and 2026-04-25 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43208)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=hungary%20election&date=2026-03-26%202026-04-25](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=hungary%20election&date=2026-03-26%202026-04-25) for 2026-04-25 is within 3 of the value at 2026-04-14. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "hungary election",
  "date": "2026-03-26 2026-04-25",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
